Overall, we can see that Semiconductor Test is still the main growth driver, Product Test is doing its thing in the background, and Robotics is basically flat. When it comes to the margins, the picture is better. Gross margin has sat around the 58% mark so far, and it should be the same in Q4. The operating margin is close to 20%, and I like the rebound back to historical levels because this is the type of operating leverage we expect from Teradyne. Earnings are in the same boat: up in Q1, down in Q2, and rising quickly in Q3 and ideally in Q4.

These movements are largely because of the slowdown and pickup in test demand over those periods, and this is how Teradyne’s business model works. With cash flow, I’d call it a solid performance. The company recorded nearly $400 million in operating cash flow over the first three quarters of the year, and management spent a huge chunk of their cash on stock buybacks and dividend payments (nearly $600 million total) in the same period. So, the company has a lot less cash now than it did at the beginning of the year, but the short-term debt amount is much higher (by $200 million, specifically). I’m not too bothered because these moves have really boosted the stock’s rise, but it is a strategy I will keep an eye on.

Q4 FY25 Preview: A Step-Change Quarter With Very Little Margin For Error

I’m looking at Q4 as an inflection quarter. Management guided revenue to $920 million to $1.0 billion, with GAAP EPS of $1.12 to $1.39. If Teradyne lands anywhere near the midpoint of that range, it will be a material step-up from the $769 million posted in Q3, and it will confirm that the Semiconductor Test rebound was more than a one-quarter snapback.

What I am watching first is where the upside comes from. I expect Semiconductor Test to be the main driver for the Q4 beat, margin expansion, and volume. This is because it supports the idea that test intensity and mix are improving in a structurally favorable way. Conversely, I would be surprised if revenue comes in strong but margins disappoint. If that happens, it means there’s a less attractive mix or more pricing pressure than the headline numbers imply.

I want some clarity around Robotics too, and we should get that in Q4. I don’t expect it to suddenly become a growth engine, but I want to see it start helping the consolidated margin. I will consider flat revenue with improving profitability as a good sign, but failing that, I think I’d just weigh it up as a long-dated option for the next few quarters.

The last thing I want to touch on is order commentary and visibility. In my view, the real question for Q4 is whether customers are still ordering into early 2026 or whether it represents a pull-forward tied to a specific ramp. Teradyne’s business has always been sensitive to those digestion phases, and a strong Q4 does not eliminate that risk.

The Market Is Pricing Teradyne As If The Cycle Never Turns

At the time of writing, Teradyne trades at ¬$232 per share, ss its market capitalization is around $35 billion on about 156–157 million shares outstanding.

The valuation is already very stretched if you only look at the trailing numbers. The stock trades at roughly ~81x trailing P/E, ~12x P/S, and well over 50x EV/EBITDA. Those are multiples that I think would apply only to asset-light, structurally recurring growth businesses.

On the other hand, even the forward multiples don’t help with the disconnect. Going off Q1–Q3 2025 actuals and Q4 guidance, Teradyne is on track to make ¬$3.0–3.1 billion in FY2025 revenue. Let us use its normalized operating margins of 18–20%. In this case, the company’s normalized net income comes out to $490–520 million, which translates to ~$3.20–3.35 in normalized EPS.

At the current share price, it means we’re looking at a forward P/E well north of 65x on earnings. That is an incredibly generous view. I think it is even more unreasonable when you remember that this is a company that has historically traded much closer to 18–22x mid-cycle earnings. If I apply a 20x multiple to a normalized EPS of ~$3.25, it gives me a fair value of roughly $65 per share, and even if I stretch the multiple to 22x, you still only get to the $70s.

In my opinion, the only way today’s valuation holds is if you assume something closer to an unreasonable bull-case outcover That would be very high AI-driven Semiconductor Test demand well into 2026, 2027, and beyond. You’d also need Robotics to become a major growth and margin contributor, and the company’s overall margins to hold near peak levels without any meaningful digestion. In that scenario, I think Teradyne’s EPS could get closer to $3.75-$4.00, and the market might justify a 22–24x multiple.

But the truth is that even that incredibly optimistic scenario does not support a >$230 stock. I used a DCF to weigh it up, going with what I consider reasonably generous assumptions.

  • A mid-double-digit revenue growth over the next decade,

  • ~20% operating margins,

  • 4-5% capex,

  • a 9% discount rate, and

  • a 3% terminal growth.

With those metrics, I got an intrinsic value in the $80 range, so clearly, the math just doesn’t stretch far enough to justify today’s price without some ridiculous assumptions.

To be clear, I’m not debating whether Teradyne is a good company or if its tailwinds are strong enough to sustain its business. It remains one of the best franchises in semiconductor testing, but quality does not eliminate cyclicality, and it does not justify paying just any price for the stock.

Risks Against Expectations

At this point, I think the biggest risk to Teradyne is expectation. I already outlined why the current stock price is unsustainable, and this isn’t how the business works (blindly assuming that demand stays consistently high). Yes, we’ve seen AI-tailwinds increase the demand in H2, and those will likely continue into FY26. The issue is that this is a cyclical industry, and if Q4 turns out to be a pull-forward instead of starting a steady run-rate around those levels, I believe the sentiment can shift quickly.

Following on from that, we have customer concentration. The biggest semiconductor companies are from whom Teradyne makes a major portion of its money, so any dips in order volume will show up quickly in the results. If that happens, it is easier to manage the volatility when the stock is at a reasonable price than if it is overpriced. The same thing goes for the margins.

I’d also pay attention to how management allocates capital moving forward. They spent over half a billion dollars on capital returns to shareholders in the first three quarters of FY25, and that has helped the stock’s rally. It’s all well and good when the business is performing, but things can also change quickly if we see any slowdown in demand. It’s not a balance sheet problem, but I see less flexibility than a year ago.

Final Thoughts

As I’ve pointed out several times in my discussion, Teradyne is a great business. That isn’t debatable. My problem is with the current valuation, given how far the market is extrapolating today’s strength into the future. The price just doesn’t work, whether you go with normalized earnings or generous assumptions about the company’s growth and margins in the near future, and I believe that the risk-reward skews very clearly to the downside.

It’s not a stock I’d chase into Q4 FY25 or Q1 FY26, regardless of how strong those quarters come in. I would almost recommend a Sell at these levels, but I’ll keep my rating at a Hold until I see Q4’s earnings results.

data.title: The Small Cap Rotation - Thoughts And How To Play It
data.description:
When we look at the small-cap stock market as a potential investment, we consider two primary factors:

  1. Valuation (current and expected), and

  2. Rates (current and expected).

Valuations give us a starting point, and rates help us understand headwind or tailwind.

Executive Summary

As of January 2026, US small-cap stocks (Russell 2000) are positioned at a pivotal juncture driven by the Federal Reserve’s pivot to monetary easing. After years of underperformance relative to large caps, small caps have entered 2026 with historically attractive valuations, trading at near 25-year lows relative to the S&P 500. The primary catalyst remains the trajectory of interest rates due to the high prevalence of floating-rate debt on small corporate balance sheets.

The Fed projects only one 25bp cut in 2026 (median dot plot: 3.4% terminal rate), though market pricing suggests 2-3 cuts. Economic growth is projected at 2.3% with core PCE inflation at 2.5%. For small caps, the key inflection depends on whether debt service relief from prior rate cuts and potential 2026 easing can offset refinancing pressures from over $300 billion in 2026 debt maturities. The base case for 2026 suggests a normalization of the yield curve and a continued, albeit gradual, reduction in short-term rates, creating a favorable setup for small-cap earnings recovery. Quality bifurcation within the Russell 2000 will be critical, as approximately 40-43% of constituents remain unprofitable.

It is important to understand the size of the small-cap market relative to the entirety of the U.S. equity market. Small caps make up ~5% of the entire market, so overweighting it (if you run against an index) doesn’t consume a lot of the portfolio assets (obviously will differ based on the amount of the overweight, but doubling it requires a 5% move from elsewhere).

Currently, the market is rotating out of large caps and into small caps; the question is: will it, or can it, continue?

Valuation

Simply looking at the P/E ratio of the Russell 2000 might give one pause, as it is near the top of its range.

What one has to realize is that this is driven by two primary factors:

  1. Unprofitable / Minimally Profitable Companies: Minimally profitable companies will inflate the level of the P/E ratio. NOTE: The Russell 2000 excludes negative earners from the calculation

  2. Earnings Growth: Earnings growth expectations for US small caps are higher than the expectations of their large-cap brethren. This will, of course, reduce the forward P/E ratio to a more manageable level (assuming the index does not rise as much as forecasted earnings).

Of course, smaller firms (and their earnings) are levered to economic growth, more specifically, domestic economic growth.

While the economy is not going gangbusters, it is putting up decent numbers, which should provide traction to small-cap companies and help underwrite the valuations.

Key Observations

  • From an absolute value perspective, small caps look overvalued, but relative to large-cap stock, they look somewhat cheap. As Hotchkis & Wiley states, “Currently small caps are trading at a forward P/E of 18x versus the S&P P/E of 24x. This 22% discount is near a historic low.” While this may be true, we believe that a valuation discount may be warranted given the characteristics of many of the constituent companies.

  • Given both economic and earnings expectations, there could be a tailwind in place for small cap companies.

  • Momentum is currently on the side of small caps as investors have been rotating out of the tech heavy large cap space.

Interest Rates

Small-cap stocks exhibit heightened sensitivity to interest rate changes due to structural leverage differences versus large caps. Approximately 38-51% of small cap debt is floating-rate versus just 7-9% for large caps. This asymmetric debt profile creates outsized vulnerability to rate changes but also substantial upside potential during easing cycles.

If we look at the expectations for FOMC moves (which will affect floating rate debt), we see that the market is currently factoring in approximately three interest rate cuts. Assuming SOFR follows suit (not sure why it wouldn’t given how it is calculated), companies with floating rate debt will see their rates 75bps lower. This, of course, assumes credit spreads stay steady despite the large number of unprofitable firms.

Observations on Interest Rates

  • Small-cap debt exposure is highly concentrated: 50% of Russell 2000 debt is held by just 10% of constituents

  • 33% of Russell 2000 companies maintain net cash positions (vs. 13% for S&P 500), highlighting dispersion

  • Nearly triple the leverage of large caps creates material debt service sensitivity

  • Companies with floating-rate exposure that benefited from sub-1% rates in 2020-2021 now face refinancing at 4-5%

  • High-yield bond spreads and leveraged loan markets remain primary funding sources for lower-rated small caps

  • Credit quality deterioration: Unprofitable companies increased from 20% (2008 crisis) to 43% (current 2025)

Tailwinds / Headwinds

Tailwinds / Catalysts

  • Earnings: The numbers are optimistic about small caps. According to FactSet, Russell 2000 companies are expected to grow earnings by 35% annually through 2027. That’s more than double the 14% projected for the S&P 500. Keep your eye on how they are developing as we roll into Q4 earnings and watch the guidance.

  • Legislative foundation: The Big Beautiful Bill could be highly stimulative to small-cap companies in 2026 (as well as consumers). The 100% bonus depreciation for certain capital investments and immediate expensing of R&D spend—as opposed to it being amortized over several years—are both attractive features of the bill, particularly for smaller companies. These new rules incentivize businesses to redirect their saved tax dollars to productive uses, which is powerful for smaller businesses. Keep your eye on the recent administrative focus on credit card rates as financials make up ~17% of the Russell 2000.

  • Made in USA: The “reshoring” movement, bringing manufacturing back to the U.S. after the supply chain disruption of 2020/2021 and the administrations focus on building out the country’s manufacturing base, is providing a tailwind for small-cap industrials. ~90% of Russell 2000 revenue generated domestically vs. 60-65% for S&P 500.

  • The Curve: Regional banks are among the primary beneficiaries of the steepening yield curve and a lighter regulatory touch (financials are over 16% of the index).

  • Artificial Intelligence: Small caps are less tied to the AI infrastructure boom. If that bubble deflates, large-cap tech could stumble while small-caps keep rising. Technology makes up ~12% of the index.

Headwinds/Risks

  • Refinancing wall: $368B maturities in 2026 may overwhelm unprofitable or overleveraged companies.

  • Fed pause risk: Market pricing 2-3 cuts, but Fed signals only one; disappointment could pressure small caps as they have greater cyclical exposure to US consumer spending, housing, and regional banking. Keep your eye on consumer spending and retail sales as well as business inventories and spending. These will front-run and sales pressures in the space.

  • Quality deterioration: 43% of the index being unprofitable is unsustainable; potential for bankruptcies and distressed exchanges. Keep your eye on bank loans and BDCs for signs of stress.

  • “Higher for longer” scenario: If inflation remains sticky above 2.5%, Fed may hold rates higher than expected. Keep your eye on CPI and PCE inflation.

  • Credit spread widening: Shift from risk-on to risk-off sentiment could widen high-yield spreads, pressuring refinancing. Watch how the credit index performs and signs of widening within investment grade and high yield.

Bottom Line

The rotation into small-cap stocks has begun, bringing values higher on the optimism of economic growth, earnings growth, and lower rates/liquidity. We believe that the rotation away from large-cap growth will continue and therefore believe that small caps will benefit. We are not advocating outsized risk exposure at this juncture, as there are a number of factors that could flip the trade. Specifically, we are keeping an eye on growth (earnings, tariffs, employment) and on rates (FOMC as well as longer duration). We believe that tactically, investors may want to nearly double the small-cap market allocation (i.e., 5% > 10%) at the expense of large caps.

How to Invest

There are a number of ways to invest in small-cap stocks. The simplest of which is by buying index funds and simply buying the “entire” small cap market.

As these funds use different benchmarks, a summary of them is necessary (know what you own):

Russell 2000 Index: The Russell 2000 Index measures the performance of the small-cap segment of the US equity universe. The Russell 2000 Index is a subset of the Russell 3000® Index, which is designed to represent approximately 98% of the investable US equity market. It includes approximately 2,000 of the smallest securities based on a combination of their market cap and current index membership. The Russell 2000 is constructed to provide a comprehensive and unbiased small-cap barometer and is completely reconstituted annually to ensure larger stocks do not distort the performance and characteristics of the true small-cap opportunity set.

The S&P 600 Index: The S&P 600 Index provides investors with a benchmark for small-sized companies in the U.S. equities market. A key differentiating factor for the S&P SmallCap 600, compared with many other small-cap benchmarks, is the earnings requirement that the S&P SmallCap 600 implements, which plays an important role in how the index defines quality. To be eligible for inclusion in the index, a company should be a U.S. company, meet market capitalization requirements, maintain a public float of at least 10% of its shares outstanding, and its most recent quarter’s earnings and the sum of its trailing four consecutive quarters’ earnings must be positive.

Dow Jones US Small Cap: The DJ US Small-Cap Index is comprised of companies not selected for the large-cap index. Companies ranked 2,000 or higher are automatically assigned to the small-cap index. Current small-cap companies ranked 3,000 or higher are selected, in descending market capitalization order, until the index contains 1,750 companies. If the index does not contain 1,750 companies after applying the buffer, the largest non-constituent companies are added until the index contains 1,750 companies.

CRSP US Small Cap Index: The CRSP US Small Cap Index represents the universe of small-capitalization companies in the U.S. equity market. The index is designed to accurately represent the small-cap segment of the U.S. equity market and deliver low turnover. The CRSP U.S. Small Cap Index measures the performance of U.S. companies that fall in the bottom 2-15% of the investable market capitalization. There is no lower limit in market capitalization, other than what is specified by investability screens.

Of all the benchmarks, the S&P 600 is the only one that requires profitability, which at some point may be important.

One-year returns:

Three-year returns:

While IWM (Russell 2000) and SCHA (DJ US Small Cap) have led in the risk/return space, there may be a benefit to the S&P 600 (IJR) given the profitability hurdle (and expense ratio). This could come into play as earnings progress or headwinds mount, as the companies will be better positioned to withstand any economic or financial strain.

data.title: VGT: A Glorious Vanguard Big-Tech ETF For The American AI Trade
data.description:
The Vanguard Information Technology ETF (VGT) is an excellent vehicle for investors wanting diversified exposure to the American AI trade. I say that because it is a relatively cost-efficient fund (0.09% expense fee) and because it has demonstrated a 10-year average annual return of 22.6%, beating its sister fund - the Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO) - by an annual average of 7.5%. Over the most recent 10-year period, that annual out-performance has resulted in a more than doubling of total returns (see chart below). Today, I'll update my Seeking Alpha coverage of VGT and explain why you should consider the fund as a long-term core technology holding for your diversified portfolio.

Investment Thesis

For years on Seeking Alpha, I have advised my followers to build a well-diversified portfolio on a foundation of a high-quality low-cost S&P 500 ETF (like VOO) and to overweight the technology sector. The S&P 500 foundation is because market research shows the vast majority of ordinary investors - and even the majority of professional money managers - do not even achieve the long-term total returns of the S&P 500. Over-weighting the technology sector is because "that's where the action is" and, in my opinion, that's where the action will be for the entire 21st Century and beyond.

In the Spring of 2021, the market was rotating out of growth and technology stocks and into "value" (energy, materials, etc.) due to rising interest rates and inflation fears. In response, I wrote the Seeking Alpha piece entitled "VGT: The IT Sector Is Out Of Favor, Which Just Might Be The Reason To Buy" because the fund's significant YTD under-performance was not rational given the superior earnings growth of the tech companies in the VGT portfolio. Indeed, since that BUY-rated article was published, VGT went on to outperform the S&P 500 by nearly 50%. Last Spring I wrote a similar BUY-rated article and VGT has since outperformed the S&P 500 by 11.5%. See "VGT: Vanguard's IT ETF - Buy The Sell-Off (Fortune Favors The Bold)". My point here is not to pat myself on the back, but to show how VGT just keeps routinely outperforming the S&P 500, year-after-year. So if your ambition is to "beat the market", VGT is a great and well-diversified vehicle to take you to your destination.

According to Vanguard, the VGT fund "Includes stocks of companies that serve the electronics and computer industries or that manufacture products based on the latest applied science." In simpler terms: VGT is a bet on American AI. And the majority of the leading American AI companies in VGT have strong global brands, are cash-rich, generate strong free-cash-flow, and will benefit from the weak U.S. dollar (-10% over the past year) when it comes to yoy quarterly earnings comparisons throughout this year.

With that as background, let's take a detailed look at the fund starting with the portfolio.

Top-10 Holdings

The top-10 holdings in VGT are shown below and were taken directly from the Vanguard VGT webpage where you can learn more detailed information about the fund:

It's no surprise that the #1 holding in VGT is the world's largest company by market-cap and that this company is the leading supplier of high-performance GPUs for the AI market. I am speaking, of course, of NVIDIA (NVDA), which has a 17.5% weight in the portfolio.

Despite the popular narrative that AI is somehow all "hype", a simple slide taken from NVDA's Q3FY26 presentation shows why investors should definitely question that assertion:

As you can see from the nearly all-black slide (similar to NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang's alligator jacket), NVIDIA grew its Q3 quarterly revenue by a whopping $21.9 billion to $57 billion (+62%) yoy. According to Reuters, NVIDIA's Q3 quarterly revenue was larger than the market-caps of all but the biggest 193 companies within the S&P 500:

That certainly isn't "hype", just the opposite: it is called "high-performance". And NVIDIA's high-octane performance is driven by its strong gross margin (73.4%) and the fact that operating expenses are growing much more slowly than revenue growth. The result is that NVIDIA has a very strong free-cash-flow profile: $22.1 billion of FCF in Q3 alone. That equates to 38.8% of total revenue and an estimated $0.90/share based on an average of 24.48 billion outstanding shares at the end of the quarter. During Q3, NVDA returned $12.7 billion to shareholders - primarily thru share buybacks given that the quarterly dividend obligation is only a penny.

NVDA's Q4 and full-year FY26 results are due out on February 25, after the market closes. The company's Q4FY26 guidance in the Q3 presentation was as follows:

As you can see from the graphic, revenue is expected to jump $8 billion on a sequential basis and gross margin is expected to rise to 75% (+160 bps on a sequential basis) as the Blackwell Ultra platform continues to ramp-up and gain manufacturing efficiencies of scale. Bottom line: expect NVDIA to deliver another strong quarter of FCF - which will likely top $25 billion for the first time (remember, that is free-cash-flow in a single quarter).

As I pointed out in a recent Seeking Alpha article (see "The Big Picture: Thoughts On Oil And AI Cross-Pollination"), investors should not be overly concerned about NVIDIA's investments in other AI companies. It is a prudent and intentional risk-mitigation strategy. In my opinion - given NVDA's strong FCF profile, investors should be concerned if NVIDIA were not investing in other AI "angles".

The #2 holding in VGT is Apple (AAPL) with a 14.9% weight. Finally, and I'll never understand what took so long, Apple and Google (GOOG) have come to an agreement (see the joint statement here) for Apple's use of Gemini AI:

... multi-year collaboration under which the next generation of Apple Foundation Models will be based on Google's Gemini models and cloud technology. These models will help power future Apple Intelligence features, including a more personalized Siri coming this year. After careful evaluation, Apple determined that Google's Al technology provides the most capable foundation for Apple Foundation Models and is excited about the innovative new experiences it will unlock for Apple users. Apple Intelligence will continue to run on Apple devices and Private Cloud Compute, while maintaining Apple's industry-leading privacy standards.

This was long-overdue in my opinion, but apparently it took Google's very successful roll-out of Gemini AI integration into its Pixel smartphones for Apple to realize it was being left behind in its most important product line (i.e. iPhones). Indeed, I predicted last October that Apple pretty much had to have Google for help with its AI (in this case, that means both "Artificial Intelligence" and "Apple Intelligence" ...) strategy - see "Google: The AI Leader OpenAI Is Still Chasing (But Will Never Catch"). That's because, at the end of the day, Apple iPhone users don't care what technology is under-the-hood, they just want their iPhones to work and they want to be able to do all the cool stuff that Google Pixel and Samsung phones can do.

That being the case, I think the Apple/Google announcement was an inflection point for both companies, but much more so for Apple. That's because unlike many big-tech companies, Apple has not dramatically increased cap-ex spending for AI, but they are getting arguably the best AI technology on the planet (i.e. Google's) for its platform. That should enable Apple to monetize its huge and loyal customer base. That should lead to a significant increase in free-cash-flow going forward. For Google, while all eyes were on the Gemini part of the announcement (which I expected), my eyes were on the "cloud technology" part of the quote above because I expect Google Cloud to be a direct beneficiary of the collaboration.

Broadcom (AVGO) is the #4 holding with a 4.5% weight. My followers know I am all-in on Broadcom (and went even further "in" after the sell-off in AVGO stock late last year) because it has what I have called the "AI Trifecta": AI compute engines (XPUs), leading-edge high-speed networking equipment, and VMWare enterprise software, which has strategic partnerships enabling full-stack support for NVDA and AMD GPUs (and, of course, Broadcom's XPUs). This gives customers excellent flexibility and enables them to mix-n-match any and all of the leading AI processors under the VMWare umbrella. See: "Broadcom: What So Many Analysts And Investors Got Wrong - Buy The Dip".

This week, Broadcom stock got hit by yet another sell-off when a report surfaced that Meta (META) was going to sign an agreement to use AMD chips. That sent the two stocks in opposite directions:

In my opinion, that is just another irrational reaction and another great buying opportunity to pick up Broadcom shares on the cheap. That's because all the major hyperscalers are buying processors from multiple vendors, and Meta's deal with AMD does not take away from the fact that Broadcom is going to start shipping XPU's for META in the 2H of this year on TSMC's leading-edge 2nm process. My point is that AMD is actually quite late to the party here because META has been a partner of Broadcom for years. See "Broadcom: The Secret Sauce Behind Its Booming XPU Business".

Note that AMD is the #6 holding in the VGT ETF with a 1.7% weight.

Memory maker Micron Technology (MU) is the #8 holding with a 1.6% weight. As you can see from this slide from Micron's Q1FY26 presentation, the company is just killing it across both its primary business units:

High-bandwidth memory ("HBM") DRAM-based solutions accounted for $10.8 billion of the quarter's total sales (79%), which was up a whopping 69% yoy and 20% qoq. Average selling prices were +20% qoq, which are indicative of a structural lack of supply. Likewise, NAND revenue of $2.7 billion (20% of total quarterly revenue) was +22% on both a yoy and qoq basis. ASPs for NAND solutions increased in the "mid-teens percentage range" qoq - again, indicative of strong demand combined with a structural lack of supply. This is very bullish for Micron moving forward.

This slide below take from the same presentation says it all, and demonstrates exactly why - for years now - I have been advising Seeking Alpha investors to be over-weight the technology sector and, specifically, to have a full-position in semiconductors:

The words in the red-box speak for themselves.

As for the portfolio as a whole, here is VGT's exposure to the various sub-sectors within IT:

As you can see from the graphic, VGT's largest exposure (32.4%) is to semiconductors. That's a good thing in my opinion.

However, I am starting to get a bit skittish on the allocation to software, which in aggregate - taking the combination of Applications & System Software exposure - accounts for ~32%, or about the same as for semiconductors. I'm skittish because after owning the iShares Expanded Tech-Software ETF (IGV) for many years, I recently sold it a couple weeks ago due to its significant under-performance versus my other tech ETFs:

I'm sure glad I did because IGV took another step lower this week. Microsoft (the #3 holding in VGT) has been trading poorly for some time now. That may be due to Google taking over OpenAI and thus Google Cloud picking up strong momentum in the hyperscaler market. Or, there may be a kernel (pun intended) of truth that software companies are at high-risk of being disrupted by automated AI code-generation. In particular, Anthropic's (ANTHRO) Claude LLM appears to be a favorite of developers for code-generation. Whatever, I got tired of IGV's lagging performance and I hope the software "issue" doesn't bleed over into VGT's excellent performance track record. It certainly is something for investors to keep an eye on.

Serious Omission

As mentioned earlier in one of the previously referenced Seeking Alpha articles, I believe that Google is currently the global-leader in AI. I say that not simply based on Google's foundational models (i.e. Gemini+ AI), but for the depth of AI across Google's entire portfolio: DeepMind, AlphaFold, the TPU product line, autonomous driving, and - of course - Google Cloud. And, Google's ability to monetize AI across its massive platform - YouTube (the #1 streamer on the planet), Google Search, and the Android OS (Chrome, Pixel smartphones, etc) - is simply unmatched in my opinion.

That being the case, and given the title of this article, Google stock - being categorized in the "Communications Services" tech sub-sector - means it is absent from VGT . That is a big loss - especially given Google's 64% gain over the past year as the market finally figured out what I have been writing about on Seeking Alpha for a couple years now: Google is the leader in AI, not OpenAI (OPENAI). The false narrative that OpenAI's chatbot (ChatGPT) would somehow "kill Google search" has been proven to be just that (a false narrative). Meantime, OpenAI doesn't have near the depth and quality of the Google platform I just described - and it's not even close.

My point here is that if you already have a full-position in Google, VGT would be an excellent core-technology ETF companion for your portfolio. The reverse is also true: if you already have a full-position in NVDIA, you can make a better choice if diversification is on your mind (like (XLC).

Performance

As mentioned earlier, VGT has a superb long-term performance track record:

The chart below compares the 10-year total returns of VGT to some of its peers: the Fidelity MSCI IT ETF (FTEC), the State Street Technology Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLK), and the Invesco QQQ Trust (QQQ):

As you can see from the chart, VGT leads the pack by a significant margin.

Basic ETF Metrics

The following bullets list some of the basic ETF metrics most any investor should consider before allocating capital to VGT:

  • Expense Fee: 0.09%
  • AUM: $130.7 billion
  • # of Stocks: 320
  • 30-day SEC Yield: 0.38%
  • P/E Ratio: 38.6x
  • Price-to-Book Ratio: 8.9x
  • Return-on-Equity: 40.7%
  • Foreign Holdings: 0%

As you can see from the table, VGT is relatively cost-efficient, but its expense fee is 6 bps above its ultra low-cost sister fund VOO. There are clearly no liquidity issues with this fund and the yield is not a consideration at all because the primary investment thesis here is clearly capital appreciation, not income.

Risks

Like practically all investments these day, VGT is not immune from the geopolitical and global economic environment, which includes President Trump's seemingly never-ending tariff war. Another existential threat would be a significant slow-down in the AI data-center build-out, whether such a slow-down is precipitated by a lack of adequate electric power capacity, low AI-monetization rates, a lack of affordable HBM memory components, or whatever.

Another risk is clearly the lofty valuation of the fund: its P/E=38.6x is a 24% premium as compared to the S&P 500's P/E=31.1x, which itself is arguably over-valued. VGT's price-to-book ratio of 8.9x is also trading at a hefty premium (59%) to the S&P 500's price-to-book ratio of 5.6x.

The reason that VGT is awarded this kind of premium is due to the ROE bullet above: 40.7%. And also due to the strong free-cash-flow profiles of its largest holdings.

Summary & Conclusion

VGT is a cost-efficient and fantastically performing fund that should be considered by most any investor as a long-term core technology holding. Google is not in the fund, and it is America-centric (i.e. 0% foreign holdings). That means the ETF is missing out on two other leading memory makers: Samsung and HK Hynix. And that is one reason I am bullish on the iShares Core MSCI Emerging Markets ETF (IEMG) and you can read my recent Seeking Alpha coverage on IEMG here. The other reason to hold IEMG is due to the falling U.S. dollar, which I expect to continue due to what in my opinion are unwise and erratic American tariff, trade, social, foreign, and energy policies combined with America's growing Federal deficit.

Yet despite the omission of Google and foreign semiconductor stocks in the ETF, I still rate VGT a BUY based on my expectations of another strong year of AI-driven earnings reports and more strong free-cash-flow for the fund's biggest holdings.

That said, given the market trading at or near all-time highs, and the drama that so often seems to revolve around American tariff and foreign policies these days, I would not go "all-in" on VGT all at once. Instead, I recommend investors be patient and dollar-cost-average into the fund to achieve their ultimate allocation percentage over a period of six months or even more (it took me two years to build a full-position QQQ). That way, you can take advantage of market volatility, make it your friend, and pick-up shares after significant sell-offs.

data.title: McDonald's: The Risk Of A Fast-Food Pricing War Is Increasing Significantly
data.description:
A lot has changed in the last 5 years. It wasn't very long ago, in 2020, when gas prices were low, grocery prices were very low, and the consensus economic view was that inflation was transitory. Today, middle and low-income consumers are dealing with historically high credit card debt levels, unaffordable housing prices, and even grocery prices that are straining many families' budgets. While the economy has been bifurcated for a while, with upper-income families and those with wealth benefiting from rising assets and investments, this divide has become much more noticeable since 2020.

One company that is more vulnerable than before to economic cycles and inflation is McDonald's (MCD). The iconic fast-food chain has raised prices significantly over just the last 5 years, and the middle and low-income consumers the company relies on are now facing tough choices, with many so-called extra value meals at McDonald's costing well over 10 dollars.

McDonald's has faced increasingly tough times over the last several years, with costs rising and many consumers facing increasingly difficult choices even with lower-end purchases.

McDonald's has offered investors total returns of just 20.86 percent over the last three years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 78.07 percent during this same time period.

I last wrote about McDonald's in September of 2025, and I rated the company a strong sell. Today, I am reiterating my rating of the fast-food chain. Recent economic reports and the company's third-quarter earnings statement show that the middle and lower-income consumers the company relies heavily on in the US and abroad are increasingly trading down and spending less at the fast-food chain. The company should face additional headwinds over the next several years despite still trading at a growth multiple. The fast-food chain looks overvalued.

McDonald's third-quarter earnings report showed the company's earnings and revenue growth have stalled, and management's plans to shift the focus to food offerings with more focus on value and prioritize cost-cutting won't likely be sufficient to drive consistent and substantive growth moving forward. The fast-food chain most recently reported earnings per share normalized actual of $3.22 and revenue actual of $7.01 billion, missing consensus expectations for earnings per share normalized actual expectations of $3.33 share and revenue actual of nearly $6.99 billion.

While McDonald's did report that same-store traffic increased 3.6 percent from last year, and US sales rose just 2.4 percent, but this number is slightly misleading since the fast-food chain is increasingly relying on discounts to bring in customers. The company noted that lower-income spending was falling notably, while some spending by higher-end consumers continued to grow at a double-digit rate. Management is planning to continue to focus on value offering in 2026, with $2.99 snack wraps being reintroduced for the first time in 9 years and an extra value meal being rolled out nationally in September. Most importantly, the company also announced that prices for items bought in combo meals would be 15 percent lower than if these items were bought individually.

McDonald's revenues and earnings growth have stagnated over the last three years, with rising prices and consumers facing increasing spending constraints in a tough economy. The average food item on the fast-food chain's menu is 40 percent higher today, and the company is also much more vulnerable to consumer spending patterns since McDonald's is selling at a much higher pricing point than the company has traditionally.

Investors should be concerned about the multiple recent reports showing that consumer spending has slowed amongst middle and low-income consumers. The fast-food chain has already been struggling to grow earnings and revenues over the last 3 years, and McDonald's margins have flatlined during this period as well.

The fast-food chain's 12-month trailing earnings per share reported in June of 2023 were $10.85, and McDonald's 12-month trailing earnings per share stated in September of 2025 were $11.72. This modest EPS growth includes share buybacks since 2023 of nearly $8 billion over this time. The fast-food chain's net margins have fallen 1 percent from the middle of 2023 to the end of the third quarter in 2025.

This is why the increasingly likely prospect of a major and extended pricing war between McDonald's and other fast-food franchises should concern investors. Domino's Pizza (DPZ) recently announced a $5 pizza, Wendy's just came out with a new $8 combo meal that claims to give customers a free cheeseburger, and Jack in the Box has cut the price of most combo meals to bring these menu items under $10. Little Caesars and White Castle came out with new discount product offerings in the last 4 months as well.

The Fed's beige book release in November showed increasing sensitivity to prices amongst working-class families, Mastercard Pulse reported notable spending increases at discount and thrift stores over the holidays, and the University of Michigan also recently noted in December that consumer sentiment is the lowest seen since 2022, when prices began to rise dramatically. Restaurant and beverage chains such as Starbucks (SBUX) and Chipotle (CMG) also reported a notable pullback in spending amongst younger consumers in their 20s over the last year. The prospect of a significant and prolonged pricing war amongst fast food restaurant chains is rising.

This is why McDonald's looks overvalued at 25.37x expected forward earnings. While the fast-food chain's 5-year average valuation is 25.84x projected forward earnings, earnings estimates for the company moving forward are much more muted. The sector median valuation is just 20.50x expected forward earnings, and some competitors, such as Wendy's, trade at far more conservative multiples. Wendy's currently trades at just 9.87x forecasted forward earnings.

McDonald's is expected to grow earnings at just 8-10 percent over the next 3 years, and these forecasts are also likely to be optimistic since the core middle and low income consumer the company relies upon continues to struggle with higher prices and slowing wage growth. Today, nearly 90 percent of the recent earnings revisions for the fast-food chain have been downward.

All investment theses have risks, and obviously, a turn in the macroeconomic environment would likely benefit McDonald's more than in previous periods of time since the company is selling at a higher historical pricing point. Trump's use of tariffs could continue to put pressure on the dollar, and the company has also benefited recently from forex moves. Pressure on the dollar would benefit the company since nearly half of the fast-food chain's revenues come from outside of the US. McDonald's is also focused on cost-cutting, as well as AI, and other forms of technology may enable these initiatives to exceed expectations.

McDonald's business model is notably different today than in previous periods of time since the company aggressively raised prices over the last 5 years. Today, the fast-food chain is more vulnerable to business cycles and changes in consumer spending. The recent success of value menu items at both McDonald's and other competitors is also increasing the prospect of a race to the bottom pricing war as well. While the fast-food chain's revenues should remain, the growth multiple this restaurant operator trades at is likely to contract moving forward.

data.title: Sturm, Ruger & Company: 'In And Out' Is One Way To Play This One In 2026
data.description:
Unlike some of my European colleagues here on Seeking Alpha, I've always taken a liking to covering some "odd" companies for the access (or lack thereof) I have to certain markets and sectors. Few companies exemplify this approach that I use for coverage better than Sturm, Ruger & Company (RGR). Recall my latest article, which you can find here.

You will note that in a very short timeframe, the company moved from below $35/share to close to $50/share, making it a very good rate of return. I did not update at the time. First of all, it was very shortly after my article, and I had other coverages to focus on. It was also, for me, a relatively small position (though that is a somewhat weak excuse). My argument was, and remains, that savvy investors should take advantage of low prices where those low prices are to be found.

In accordance with my strategy, I also added a few more shares when the company dropped to below $30/share (that's where I had my brokerage alert for this company). The reason for this drop and rise is discussed further in the article - we have high-level moves by a Luxembourg-based company that is subject to speculation (Beretta Holdings), and it is important for shareholders to be aware of.

You may argue that this company is very small. You may argue, besides, that RGR is subject to significant political volatility and risk, given what it does. Also, that it's not in any way a "big" company given its market cap, and that the risks, therefore, are significant.

I would counter that some of these points are indeed well-put and well-made, but would then add that as an investor, I do not make political considerations beyond the impact those politics may have, instead focusing on the financial turnout. Anyone investing in anything (even something like Coca-Cola (KO), or Nestle (NSRGY) needs to make political risk considerations, needs to look at the world through eyes weighing "different" things). Politics and its impacts are just one of them. I firmly pride myself on my ability to stay level-headed in what, in my view, is more of a turmoil than even COVID-19 was. The political realities, the tariff risks, and the current situation that we (by 'we', I mean people living in Europe, parts of Asia, and NA) are in are things we have not faced since the end of the Cold War.

Is it then strange that I should focus on a company like Sturm, Ruger & Company and the appeal the products might have, as well as the company might have? Is it so different from covering, say, a European defense manufacturer?

I think not.

Sturm, Ruger & Company - Upside and appeal following a rise, dip, and rise again

RGR is, judging by article stats, probably one of my least-followed article series on Seeking Alpha. This does not faze me either - I take pride also in covering companies that others seemingly do not care about, but that are significant.

Despite its small market cap and undercovered status, RGR is one of the most significant firearms manufacturers in all of the US. It's loved for the quality of its products, which are hardy and "rugged" - and that sort of quality is something I wish would be brought to more products here. Based on data by BATFE, or the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms, and Explosives, RGR ranks or has ranked high (usually #1 or #2) by number of products produced. Its portfolio includes iconic products, such as the 10/22 (6+M units sold), the Redhawk, the Blackhawk, and other rifles, and the Mark Series, which I actually shot when I was living in the States years ago.

Sturm, Ruger & Company, despite its limited market cap and comparative size, should not be underestimated. Its recent acquisition of Marlin, which I covered in recent articles as well, has expanded its operations to more classic lever-action products as well. It has products spanning from entry-level and budget to high-end.

Furthermore, its fundamentals are beyond merely "solid" because of its near-complete lack of debt. The company's long-term debt is at a whopping 0.45% to capital and traditionally has low or no debt. Traditional metrics, given the $600M market cap and volatile end markets and political realities, cannot be accurately used to forecast this. Much like a surfer riding waves, I believe the focus should be on your "entry" and the process itself. Ups and downs should not worry you if you are interested here. The only fundamental issue that could discourage me in the long term would be something high-level against firearm ownership in the US - and the likelihood of that is, in my view, extremely remote.

This article takes into consideration both the recent 3Q25, as well as the full-year results, which are relatively close (an earnings preview, in other words). The 3Q25 results were negative relative to YoY. Losses amount to $2.1M (that's pre-tax, affected by a $0.19 EPS tax benefit - the net loss was higher, about $17.2M), but this was driven by acquisitions, the Hebron facility in July, as well as increased costs, sales/marketing spend, and internal factors. Looking at the 9M period instead of the quarterly YoY, the company's sales were actually up slightly - even if earnings here, due to the trends mentioned above, were still negative relative to positive earnings for 9M24.

Internal efficiencies are ongoing. The company reduced the number of models offered and introduced organizational restructuring, which is part of the negative results we're seeing.

At the same time, the dividend is being maintained - 40% of net income, which was paid back on November 28th (17th November is the record date). The stability of the dividend (if not the amount of payout) is valuable.

It would be wrong to call the 3Q25 period a good quarter - I would characterize it as a transition quarter more than anything else. Positive signs that the company's plans are working are found in the top-line results, with margins and earnings impacted by the above-mentioned restructuring and internal changes. Profitability is the next focal point now that the organization is slowly becoming aligned with the new reality.

The increased positive outlook for RGR is justified by sales being stable (both Marlin and Centerfire Rifles), with distributor inventory numbers being positive. Finished goods were down 15,500, while inventories on the distribution side increased by over 4,000, implying demand. As mentioned above, the company's leverage, from any perspective, is one of its foundational strengths. It has a current ratio of 3.5x. The current ratio measures short-term debt payments - anything above 1x is good, so 3.5x is beyond just 'solid' in my estimate.

I don't expect the current level of CapEx to be maintained over time, but it's likely that we'll see CapEx elevation for 4Q25 as well. The company, aside from dividend payments, continued to buy back shares at an attractive share price cost of an average of $35/share, above my average cost basis for RGR following my buys below or at $30/share.

The reason I believe that RGR is going to outperform is a combination of the aforementioned financial strength, new products, the Marlin M&A, the resulting product portfolio, and the current political situation. I've grown, over the past few years, very positive on companies that have no or little debt. What's also important to me is the demand for the company's new products, like the Rifle Generation II, and the LC, which was 34% of sales for 3Q. This conveys demand for the company's products. While it's hard to measure company loyalty, I would argue that the company has a long-lived and loyal following, committed to its products.

This gives us a good foundation for investment - and because of this, I expect a continued upside.

Valuation for RGR - Upside in 2026E

I had a fair value of $72/share, with a PT of $45/share. The company went above this, and I did a short-term rotation above $45/share in accordance with that target, because I believed the surge to be short-term oriented. This proved to be correct. I was perhaps a bit exuberant on the fair value side of things, so I'm lowering that to about $65/share, believing in a long-term AEPS potential of about $2-3. What's clear to me is that the current earnings, or the 2025E earnings, which are set to go negative, are hardly indicative of long-term stable earnings potential.

What needs to be discounted for is the company's volatile trends. Forecasting RGR is close to impossible, if we judge by historical accuracy.

I also believe the new CEO and efficiency are likely to lead to better margins and returns, and that the restructuring costs we're seeing now (which I have already accounted for in my longer-term targets), will put the company on a trajectory of outperformance for the longer timeframe.

At the same time, I believe it's crucial for investors to be flexible and agile with an investment like this. In the event of a short-term spike, such as we had not that long ago, it's crucial to be able to revisit and look at your targets, and be open (if this is in your approach), enjoy some short-term returns.

It's equally important not to panic if and when (as seems likely to continue) the company sees earnings and share-price-related volatility.

I believe 2026-2027E will be a turnaround due to launches of new products, including the Gen II Scout model, the 10/22 PC carbine, and new SBR's. I further believe the company's appeal is no longer as unclear to the market, given Beretta's significant stake in the company, leading to RGR responding with what in the business is known as a "poison pill" to prevent takeover of the newly-restructured business.

Recent news includes the threat of a proxy fight. What does this mean? In the context of this, it means (as the news says), that Beretta is looking to nominate board members ahead of the AGM. In essence, a proxy fight is a battle for corporate control (makes sense, given what Beretta is attempting to do), by replacing either parts or the entire BoD. If Beretta manages to get others on "their side", we could expect this to heat up. However, since RGR has adopted the above-mentioned poison pill, it makes it a lot harder for Beretta to move. Once the pill is triggered, existing shareholders, excluding Beretta, are granted purchasing rights either at massive discounts or even for free - which could make the acquirers' (Beretta's) percentage suddenly worth much less.

It's not an immunization, and the current plan is a short-term one, with a current expiration of October 2026, so in about 9 months. We'll see what happens.

2026E is therefore set to be interesting for the company. Despite the interest and changes, I do not shift my PT. Risks are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Recent results for the company highlight operational and execution risks for RGR. The company is very volatile (in terms of earnings, not as much in terms of overall revenue development), and this is something you need to consider and include in your "pricing" for the business, meaning your estimates.

The obvious political risks related to firearms misuse, which amount to potential regulatory and legal risks, need to be included as well, though I believe these, in the American market, are always manageable, despite significant litigation in the US market.

Valuation risks are related to how, in turn, the company is valued - meaning that P/E is a tricky metric to use here due to the significant volatility in earnings. I would therefore make it clear to use different metrics for estimating its fair value.

Lastly, note the poison poll response to Beretta above. The ultimate turnout for what'll happen here will have to be seen with time, but I believe shareholders should expect volatility.

At the current price, I consider the company attractive enough for an investment and rate it a "BUY" with a continued PT of $45/share.

Thesis

Despite choppy and volatile trends in the underlying markets, political risks, and a relatively small (seen in terms of market cap) size, I believe RGR to be a very solid company. Investors could point to increased legal and political risk - firearm and gun/rifle ownership is never an easy question in any country (not even, I argue, in the US), but this is a solid bet if you're bullish on that. I am, as of 2025, bullish on RGR. While I am far from the US (geographically based elsewhere), I can see the appeal of this company at the right price.

I put the company at a fair value of at least $65 (meaning the price I'd say the company is worth long term), with a PT of $45/share, meaning what I believe the company should be bought under. I bought and sold RGR recently, which reflects the fact that I am open to taking profits when this makes sense. At this time and at this price, I believe RGR to be a solid "BUY" - but certainly not one of the safer ones out there.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 1 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 4/5 "stars" here, which is enough for an attractive thesis for the company. Based on this, and a price target of $45/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Buy" rating as of January 2026.
